title: Section 80C Last-Minute Optimization
context: Priya earns Rs 12L per year under the old tax regime. March 10 — FY ends in 3 weeks. She has already invested Rs 60K in EPF. She has Rs 90K available cash and wants to minimize tax before the financial year closes. What should she invest in?

options.text: Invest all Rs 90K in ELSS mutual fund (3-year lock-in)
options.brief: Full 80C benefit plus potential 12-16% equity returns. Lowest lock-in among 80C instruments.

options.text: Open a PPF account and deposit Rs 90K
options.brief: Government-backed, 7.1% p.a., EEE status. 15-year lock-in. Safe but illiquid.

options.text: Buy an LIC endowment policy with Rs 90K premium
options.brief: Qualifies for 80C but gives only 4-6% returns. Mixes insurance with investment.

options.text: Keep cash in savings account and file taxes without 80C optimization
options.brief: Miss Rs 90K of deductions entirely. Pay Rs 27K+ extra tax with no benefit.

ground_truth.law_reference: Income Tax Act 1961, Section 80C(2)(xiii) — ELSS qualifies. Limit Rs 1.5L. LTCG above Rs 1L taxed at 10% after 3 years.

title: New vs Old Tax Regime — Which Saves More?
context: Arjun got a salary hike. His taxable income is now Rs 10L per year. He typically claims Rs 80C deduction Rs 1.5L, health insurance 80D Rs 25K, and HRA Rs 1.05L. Total deductions Rs 2.8L. His employer is asking him to declare his tax regime for the year. Which regime should he choose?

options.text: Switch to New Tax Regime — pay Rs 54,600 tax
options.brief: Saves Rs 62,400 vs old regime at this income and deduction level. Simpler, no proof needed.

options.text: Stay on Old Tax Regime — continue claiming all deductions
options.brief: Pays Rs 1,17,000. Correct if deductions exceed Rs 3.75L breakeven — they don't here.

options.text: Ask HR to restructure salary with more allowances under old regime
options.brief: Valid strategy but adds complexity without benefit at this income level.

options.text: Declare old regime but don't submit any investment proofs
options.brief: Old regime without proofs gives no deductions — worst of both worlds.

ground_truth.law_reference: Income Tax Act 1961, Section 115BAC — New Regime rates 2024-25. Standard deduction Rs 75K allowed in new regime.

title: Advance Tax — October Deadline for Freelancer
context: Rohan is salaried at Rs 8L per year and also earns Rs 4L from freelance work this financial year. TDS of Rs 10K has been deducted on freelance payments. He is in October. His estimated total tax liability on Rs 12L gross is Rs 1,44,000. What should he do about advance tax?

options.text: Pay advance tax by September 15 deadline — 45% of net tax liability
options.brief: September deadline passed. Pay catch-up installment now covering cumulative 45% by September.

options.text: Pay 75% of net tax liability before December 15 installment deadline
options.brief: Correct action in October — catch up to 75% cumulative by December 15. Interest accrues on missed September installment.

options.text: Wait until March 31 and pay everything at once as self-assessment tax
options.brief: Legal but attracts 234B and 234C interest penalties for missing installments.

options.text: Do nothing — freelance income is not taxable if under Rs 5L total
options.brief: Completely wrong. Rs 12L total income is well above the basic exemption. Tax evasion.

ground_truth.law_reference: Income Tax Act 1961, Section 208 (advance tax mandatory if liability exceeds Rs 10K), Section 234C (installment schedule: 15% by Jun 15, 45% by Sep 15, 75% by Dec 15, 100% by Mar 15).

title: Emergency Fund — Where to Keep Rs 3 Lakhs
context: Meera has saved Rs 3L as her emergency fund. It is sitting in her salary account earning 3.5% interest. She wants to keep it accessible within 24 hours but also earn better returns. She has been offered three alternatives by her bank relationship manager. What should she do?

options.text: Move to a liquid mutual fund (T+1 redemption, ~6.5-7% p.a.)
options.brief: Best risk-return for emergency fund. Redeemable within 1 business day. Better than savings rate.

options.text: Split: Rs 50K in savings account, Rs 2.5L in 30-day recurring FD
options.brief: Good. FD rate higher than savings. But 30-day FD has premature withdrawal penalty risk.

options.text: Invest entire Rs 3L in a 5-year FD for maximum interest
options.brief: Defeats the purpose of an emergency fund. Money locked for 5 years.

options.text: Invest Rs 3L in ELSS for tax saving and keep nothing liquid
options.brief: ELSS has 3-year lock-in. Emergency fund becomes completely inaccessible.

ground_truth.law_reference: Liquid mutual funds: SEBI categorization — maturity up to 91 days. Returns taxed at slab rate if held less than 3 years.

title: Section 80D — Health Insurance Premium Deduction
context: Kiran pays Rs 18K annual premium for his own family health insurance and Rs 32K for his senior citizen parents' health insurance. He is in the 30% tax bracket under the old regime. His CA asked him how much 80D deduction he can claim this year.

options.text: Claim Rs 18K for self and Rs 32K for senior citizen parents — total Rs 50K deduction
options.brief: Correct. Self limit Rs 25K (Rs 18K used), senior citizen parents limit Rs 50K (Rs 32K used).

options.text: Claim only Rs 25K — the standard 80D limit for self
options.brief: Wrong. Missing the additional Rs 50K deduction available for senior citizen parents.

options.text: Claim Rs 25K for self and Rs 25K for parents — total Rs 50K
options.brief: Wrong limit for senior citizen parents. Parents get Rs 50K limit, not Rs 25K.

options.text: Claim Rs 75K — maximum 80D deduction allowed under any circumstance
options.brief: Overclaiming. Actual premiums paid are Rs 50K so deduction cannot exceed Rs 50K.

ground_truth.law_reference: Income Tax Act 1961, Section 80D — Self/family limit Rs 25K. Senior citizen parents limit Rs 50K. Combined maximum Rs 75K if both senior.

title: Term Insurance vs Endowment Policy
context: Vikram is 32, married with one child, earns Rs 15L per year. He has no life insurance. His bank is offering him a Rs 50L endowment policy at Rs 1.8L annual premium for 20 years. An online insurer offers a Rs 1 Crore term insurance at Rs 12K annual premium. He has Rs 1.8L available for insurance. What should he do?

options.text: Buy Rs 1Cr term insurance at Rs 12K and invest remaining Rs 1.68L in mutual funds
options.brief: Best risk coverage at lowest cost. Separate insurance from investment.

options.text: Buy the Rs 50L endowment policy at Rs 1.8L premium
options.brief: Half the coverage at 15x the cost. Returns of 4-5% are below inflation.

options.text: Buy both — endowment for guaranteed returns and term for pure risk
options.brief: Pays Rs 1.92L total. Term part is right but endowment is still wasteful.

options.text: Skip insurance — put entire Rs 1.8L in mutual funds instead
options.brief: No life cover for dependents. If Vikram dies, family has no financial safety net.

ground_truth.law_reference: IRDAI regulations. Insurance is risk coverage — not investment. Term premium qualifies for 80C deduction under Section 80C(2)(a).

title: Credit Card Debt vs Investments — What to Do First?
context: Sanya has Rs 80K outstanding on her credit card at 36% annual interest. She also has Rs 80K in a liquid mutual fund earning about 6.5% per year. Her salary is Rs 6L per year. Her HR keeps telling her to stay invested. Should she redeem the mutual fund to clear the credit card?

options.text: Redeem the liquid fund immediately and clear the credit card in full
options.brief: Saves 36% interest vs earning 6.5%. Net gain of 29.5% annualized. Always clear high-cost debt first.

options.text: Pay only the minimum due on the card and keep the mutual fund
options.brief: Paying 36% on Rs 80K while earning 6.5% on Rs 80K. Net loss of 29.5% annually.

options.text: Transfer the credit card balance to a 0% EMI scheme and pay over 6 months
options.brief: Valid if processing fee is low. But if liquid fund is already available, full clearance is simpler.

options.text: Take a personal loan at 14% to pay the credit card — lower interest rate
options.brief: Reduces interest but adds loan processing steps when a better fund asset already exists.

ground_truth.law_reference: No specific statute. Basic personal finance: guaranteed return of avoided interest always beats uncertain investment return.

title: NPS vs EPF for Additional Retirement Contribution
context: Deepak is 35, earns Rs 18L per year, already maxing out EPF and 80C limit. His CA told him he can get an additional Rs 50K deduction under Section 80CCD(1B) by contributing to NPS Tier-1. He has Rs 50K available and is deciding between NPS Tier-1 and a 5-year FD.

options.text: Invest Rs 50K in NPS Tier-1 — claim Rs 50K additional deduction under 80CCD(1B)
options.brief: Unique Rs 50K deduction OVER and ABOVE the Rs 1.5L 80C limit. Saves Rs 15K tax immediately.

options.text: Put Rs 50K in a 5-year tax-saving FD under 80C
options.brief: Wrong — 80C limit already maxed via EPF. FD deduction will not apply. No tax benefit.

options.text: Invest Rs 50K in PPF — better liquidity than NPS
options.brief: PPF falls under 80C which is already maxed. No additional benefit. PPF more liquid than NPS but no extra deduction.

options.text: Keep Rs 50K in savings — no investment needed beyond EPF
options.brief: Leaves Rs 15K tax benefit on the table. Rs 50K in savings earns 3.5%, Rs 50K in NPS grows tax-free.

ground_truth.law_reference: Income Tax Act 1961, Section 80CCD(1B) — Rs 50K additional deduction for NPS Tier-1, over and above Section 80C Rs 1.5L limit. At withdrawal: 60% corpus tax-free, 40% mandatorily used for annuity.

title: Home Loan Prepayment vs SIP Investment
context: Rahul received a Rs 3L bonus. He has a home loan at 8.75% interest with Rs 22L outstanding and 12 years remaining. His SIP portfolio is averaging 12% CAGR over the past 5 years. His loan EMI is Rs 25K per month. Should he prepay the loan or invest the bonus?

options.text: Invest the entire Rs 3L in mutual fund SIP — higher long-term returns
options.brief: 12% equity return vs 8.75% loan cost. BUT loan interest partially deductible under 24(b). Net math favors investing.

options.text: Prepay Rs 3L on the home loan — reduces principal and interest burden
options.brief: Effective return = 8.75% guaranteed. But after 24(b) deduction, effective loan cost is lower. Investing likely wins.

options.text: Split Rs 1.5L prepayment and Rs 1.5L investment
options.brief: Balanced approach. Valid if risk tolerance is moderate. Not optimal but not harmful.

options.text: Use entire Rs 3L to buy gold as a hedge
options.brief: Gold long-run return is 8-9%. Neither clears debt efficiently nor beats equity. Poor use of bonus.

ground_truth.law_reference: Income Tax Act 1961, Section 24(b) — Rs 2L annual deduction on home loan interest for self-occupied property. Effective post-tax loan rate = 8.75% x (1 - 0.30) = 6.1%.

title: LTCG on Equity Shares — To Sell or Not in March
context: Pooja bought shares in April 2022 and they have gained Rs 1.8L in value by March 2025. She has not sold any other equity this financial year. The LTCG exemption is Rs 1.25L per year. She is trying to decide whether to sell before or after March 31.

options.text: Sell Rs 1.25L worth of gains now to use this year's exemption, sell rest in April for next year's exemption
options.brief: Tax harvesting. Use this year's Rs 1.25L exemption fully. Rebuy immediately to reset cost basis. Saves Rs 6.875K.

options.text: Sell everything now and pay LTCG tax on Rs 55K above exemption
options.brief: Valid. Pays Rs 6,875 tax. Less efficient than splitting across two financial years.

options.text: Wait and sell after April 1 — use next year's fresh exemption for the full Rs 1.8L gain
options.brief: Wastes this year's Rs 1.25L exemption. Next year gain may exceed the exemption limit.

options.text: Do not sell — hold indefinitely and gains are tax free
options.brief: Completely wrong. LTCG above Rs 1.25L is always taxable at 12.5%. No permanent exemption exists.

ground_truth.law_reference: Income Tax Act 1961, Section 112A — LTCG on listed equity above Rs 1.25L taxed at 12.5% with no indexation. Finance Act 2024 raised exemption from Rs 1L to Rs 1.25L.
